title: "Sapphire Crown Puzzle v11027"

description: |
  At 22 : 05 the **Sapphire Crown** vanished from its sealed pedestal.  
  Four badge-holders (1 2 3 4) were recorded nearby.  
  • Exactly **one** suspect lies in *all* of their statements; the other three are perfectly truthful.  
  • Exactly **one** suspect is guilty.  
  • The universal liar and the culprit are **different** people.

  Suspect 1  
    (1) $L2 \rightarrow \lnot G1$  
    (2) $T2 \rightarrow P4$  
    (3) $P3 \oplus L4$  
    (4) $S_{2,3} \rightarrow G2$  
    (5) $G2 \rightarrow \lnot L4$

  Suspect 2  
    (1) $\lnot L1$  
    (2) $L3 \rightarrow (\,P3 \land \lnot P2\,)$  
    (3) $G2 \oplus L4$  
    (4) \,$\lnot P4 \rightarrow L4$  
    (5) $(\,P1 \lor P3\,) \rightarrow \lnot G1$

  Suspect 3  
    (1) $\lnot P3$  
    (2) $T4 \rightarrow P2$  
    (3) $L2 \lor G1$  
    (4) $S_{1,1} \rightarrow L1$  
    (5) $G3$

  Suspect 4  
    (1) $\lnot G4$  
    (2) $P4$  
    (3) $L3$  
    (4) $\lnot P2$  
    (5) $P3 \land S_{2,3}$

  Notation  
  $Lx$: “x lies in every statement.” $Tx$: “x tells only truths.”  
  $Gx$: “x is guilty.” $Px$: “x was present at 22 : 05.”  
  $S_{i,j}$: *statement j of suspect i is true.*  
  $\lnot$ negation $\land$ and $\lor$ or $\oplus$ exclusive-or $\rightarrow$ implication

question: "Who stole the Sapphire Crown? Return a single digit (1–4)."

answer: >
  explanation: |
    Assume in turn that each suspect is the sole liar.

    • Case $L1$: statement 1(1) forces $L2$, giving two liars. Reject.  
    • Case $L2$: 2(2) becomes false only if both $L3$ and $T4$ are true, which adds another liar. Reject.  
    • Case $L3$: all five of 3’s formulas are false, yet every formula of 1, 2, 4 can be satisfied with  
      $G2,\;L3,\;P3,\;P4,\;\lnot P1,\;\lnot P2,\;L1=L2=L4=\text{false}$.  
      This gives exactly one liar (3) and exactly one guilty (2). Consistent.  
    • Case $L4$: statement 4(1) would be false, so $G4$ true; but 4 also asserts $P4$ and $L3$, forcing
      contradictions with other truthful suspects. Reject.

    The single consistent scenario is  
    $L3$ **and** $G2$.

  final: '2'
